g.data-narration: Welcome to Memory Management! This module covers how operating systems efficiently manage computer memory.
Memory management handles primary memory allocation, tracks usage, and ensures efficient utilization while protecting processes from interference.
g.text: Manages primary memory (RAM), moves processes between

g.data-narration: The memory hierarchy shows different storage types from fast expensive registers to slow cheap tertiary storage, balancing speed and cost.
Logical addresses are generated by CPU and unique to each process, while physical addresses refer to actual RAM locations.
Single contiguous allocation gives entire memory to one process. Simple but wastes memory and prevents multiprogramming.
Fixed partitioning divides memory into predetermined partitions, each holding one process. Enables multiprogramming but suffers from internal fragmentation.
g.text: but limit process size to partition boundaries

g.data-narration: Dynamic partitioning creates partitions exactly matching process sizes, eliminating internal fragmentation but causing external fragmentation requiring compaction.
Internal fragmentation wastes space within allocated blocks. External fragmentation scatters free memory, making allocation difficult despite sufficient total free space.
Compaction consolidates scattered free memory by moving processes together. Though expensive computationally, it reduces external fragmentation effectively.
g.text: Compaction moves processes to consolidate free memory

g.data-narration: First-fit finds first adequate hole quickly. Best-fit minimizes waste but searches extensively. Worst-fit leaves large fragments but is rarely efficient.
Base and limit registers protect processes by checking every memory access is within legal bounds, preventing unauthorized memory access.
Swapping moves processes between memory and disk temporarily, enabling more processes than physical memory can hold at once.
g.text: Swap time depends on disk transfer rate. Process moved back when memory available.

g.data-narration: The Memory Management Unit is hardware that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses, providing memory protection and enabling virtual memory.
In summary, memory management uses allocation methods, handles fragmentation, and protects memory through hardware like base/limit registers and MMU.
